channel.title: Projects on Aaron Tohuvavohu
channel.description: Recent content in Projects on Aaron Tohuvavohu

item.title: NASA Swift Automation Initiative
item.description:
I will be presenting current work on this topic at the Advanced Computing and Analysis Techniques in Physics Research conference in Seattle in August. The relevant abstract is below. This work is ongoing, check back for updates.

Improving Science Yield for NASA Swift with Automated Planning Technologies

The Swift Gamma-Ray Burst Explorer is a uniquely capable mission, with three on-board instruments and rapid slewing capabilities. It often serves as a fast-response space observatory for everything from gravitational-wave counterpart searches to cometary science. Swift averages 125 different observations per day, and is consistently over-subscribed, responding to about one-hundred Target of Oportunity (ToO) requests per month from the general astrophysics community, as well as co-pointing and follow-up agreements with many other observatories. Since launch in 2004, the demands put on the spacecraft have grown consistently in terms of number and type of targets as well as schedule complexity. To facilitate this growth, various scheduling tools and helper technologies have been built by the Swift team to continue improving the scientific yield of the Swift mission. In this study, we examine various approaches to the automation of observation schedules for the Swift spacecraft, comparing the efficiency and quality of these tools to each other and to the output of well-trained human Science Planners. Because of the computational complexity of the scheduling task, no automation tool has been able to produce a plan of equal or higher quality than that produced by a well-trained human, given the necessary time constraints. We detail here several approaches towards achieving this goal of surpassing human quality schedules using classical optimization and algorithmic techniques, as well as machine learning and recurrent neural network (RNN) methods. We then quantify the increased scientific yield and benefit to the wider astrophysics community that would result from the further development and adoption of these technologies.

item.title: Issues with First Quantization Quantum Mechanics on Curved Space-times
item.description:
During my senior year at Reed I worked with Prof. Joel Franklin on a thesis project. It started as a humble attempt to describe the gravitational interaction of photons that behaved according to the quantum mechanical wave-function for single photons (see here) and ended with an investigation of the reasons for the absence of bound states when treating the Dirac equation around black holes with an event horizon. Along the way I studied a variety of tools and topics in math and physics including, among others, the spectral theory of differential operators, the RAGE and ergodic theorems (thanks to Prof. Tom Wieting), self-adjoint extensions, spinors, the BTZ black hole, the tetrad formalism, gravitational waves, Hawking radiation, and Chandrasekhar separation.

The thesis at Reed is supposed to be accessible to one’s peers; that is, fellow senior undergraduates. Indeed, most incoming physics seniors (including myself) peruse theses from years past for ideas and inspiration in the search for a thesis topic. Joel helped me realize that a very compelling story could be told with some of the work I had done throughout the year: a story about a search for bound states. For the sake of the narrative there is much that I did throughout the year that is not contained in the final document. The tone throughout, but especially in the introduction and conclusion, is very casual and is meant to set the stage for the story. This thesis is not structured so much as a presentation of research findings as it is a narrative of intellectual discovery and struggle. My hope is that by providing the undergraduate reader with an understanding of the reasons why I chose to take a certain path through the space of possible research programs they will be more engaged and will come away with an understanding of not only why the Dirac equation does not admit bound states in certain black hole space-times, but also what it means to do research in theoretical physics. It is also my hope that readers with a more advanced background will find content of interest in this thesis, as several intriguing questions and points are raised, including: equivalent notions of bound states, co-ordinate invariant spectral properties of differential operators, and why closed geodesics are lost when the Dirac equation is introduced.

This attempt at presenting material that most would consider abstruse in an engaging, and hopefully different, manner has also awakened in me a desire to become a better writer and communicator of science.

item.title: Simulating Quantum Algorithms
item.description:
TL;DR: Check this out.

This project began at the 2016 Wolfram Summer Science School, where I was a participant. We had been told to arrive with a project idea in mind, and having just finished working my way through Maximilian Schlosshauer’s fantastic book Decoherence and the Quantum-to-Classical Transition, I naturally had the idea of simulating the decoherence of some quantum system, and perhaps demonstrating how the decohering timescale changes for systems of different size. My thoughts on this matter were also stimulated by a very interesting, but ultimately dissapointing, talk that Dr. Michael Kellman gave at Reed in the Fall of 2015 where he presented the results of this paper. While he didn’t talk about decoherence, his talk inspired in me an interest in simulating quantum systems, and my fascination with Max’s book demanded a need to know more about decoherence (which hadn’t been mentioned at all in my undergraduate quantum courses).

However, the moment I entered the room for my project meeting with Dr. Stephen Wolfram, he already had a different idea in mind. The idea was to build functionality into Mathematica for easily building and simulating quantum algorithms that was powerful enough for researchers to use and simple enough to be used as an educational tool. At the time I wasn’t aware that only a month earlier IBM had launched their Quantum Experience platform, but I’m sure Stephen was. I immediately realized that this was a great idea that would allow me to explore my interest in simulating quantum systems, while also forcing me to think hard about design and usability (something scientists aren’t known for), and that would necessitate a solid grasp of the subject matter. I began immediately, and spent the next 3 weeks working in a frenzy. You can read about the state of the project by the end of the summer school here. Suffice to say, it worked.

After the summer school I was asked to continue work on this project to extend the functionality and prepare it for ultimate inclusion in the Wolfram Language technology stack.
